name: Vidyaranya
dates_notes: Vijayanagara court records; dual identity as MńĀdhavńĀcńĀrya (administrator) and VidyńĀraŠĻáya (sannyńĀsin) attested.

dates_evidence.description: Vijayanagara royal records attest his administrative role and sannyńĀsa-name.
Manuscript colophons of the Pa√ĪcadaŇõńę, Sarva-DarŇõana-SaŠĻÉgraha, and other works confirm dual identity.

lineage_polemical.note: VidyńĀraŠĻáya's Sarva-DarŇõana-SaŠĻÖgraha (under the MńĀdhavńĀcńĀrya identity) surveys the Pratyabhij√ĪńĀ-darŇõana of Utpaladeva and Abhinavagupta as one of the principal non-VedńĀntic darŇõanas, treating it with disciplined fairness rather than as a cursory rival.
The Pratyabhij√ĪńĀ chapter of the Sarva-DarŇõana-SaŠĻÖgraha takes Abhinavagupta's articulation of svńĀtantrya, ńĀbhńĀsa-vńĀda, and pratyabhij√ĪńĀ as its primary source for the school.

core_thesis: VidyńĀraŠĻáya stands at the institutional and philosophical apex of fourteenth-century Advaita. As MńĀdhavńĀcńĀrya he served the early Vijayanagara court and, with his brother SńĀyaŠĻáa, presided over a comprehensive scholastic project (so the Vijayanagara court records and the colophons of the *Sarva-DarŇõana-SaŠĻÉgraha* and SńĀyaŠĻáa's Vedic *bhńĀŠĻ£yas*; cf. Sastri, *VidyńĀraŠĻáya: A Study*, 1972); as VidyńĀraŠĻáya he became ŇöaŠĻÖkarńĀcńĀrya of SŠĻõŠĻÖgeri (so the *SŠĻõŠĻÖgeri Guru-ParamparńĀ* records). Doctrinally he is firmly within the VivaraŠĻáa line: Brahman is the locus of *bhńĀva-rŇępńĀ-anirvacanńęya-avidyńĀ* ([*Pa√ĪcadaŇõńę* I.15‚Äď16](cite://vidyaranya/panchadashi/I.15-16), *mńĀyńĀ* and *avidyńĀ* as the two faces of Brahman's *prakŠĻõti*; cf. PrakńĀŇõńĀtman, *Pa√Īca-PńĀdikńĀ-VivaraŠĻáa* on [*Brahma-SŇętra* 1.1.1](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.1)), and the *jńęva* is *cidńĀbhńĀsa* ([*Pa√ĪcadaŇõńę* VI.1‚Äď10](cite://vidyaranya/panchadashi/VI.1-10), the *citra-dńępa* chapter). His distinctive synthetic contribution is twofold. First, he treats the two competing technical analyses of the *jńęva* ‚ÄĒ *pratibimba-vńĀda* (Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman, [*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 1.319](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.319); PrakńĀŇõńĀtman, *Pa√Īca-PńĀdikńĀ-VivaraŠĻáa* on 1.1.1: *jńęva* as reflection of Brahman in *avidyńĀ*) and *avaccheda-vńĀda* (VńĀchaspati's *BhńĀmatńę* on *Brahma-SŇętra-BhńĀŠĻ£ya* 1.1.1, the *jńęva* as Brahman delimited by *antaŠł•karaŠĻáa*) ‚ÄĒ as genuine *prakriyńĀ*-level alternatives within a single VivaraŠĻáa framework ([*Pa√ĪcadaŇõńę* VI.6‚Äď10](cite://vidyaranya/panchadashi/VI.6-10) and VIII.5‚Äď7), rather than positions one of which must be wrong. Second, he gives a systematic exposition of *jńęvanmukti* as a precise technical state, not a metaphor (*Jńęvanmukti-Viveka*, *prathama-prakaraŠĻáa*): *avidyńĀ* has been removed, *prńĀrabdha-karma* sustains the body until exhausted ([*Jńęvanmukti-Viveka* I.5](cite://vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/I.5); cf. ŇöaŠĻÖkara, *Brahma-SŇętra-BhńĀŠĻ£ya* 4.1.15), and *vńĀsanńĀ-kŠĻ£aya* and *manonńĀŇõa* are required for the cessation of residual mental activity ([*Jńęvanmukti-Viveka* III.1‚Äď10](cite://vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/III.1-10), drawing on *Yoga-VńĀsiŠĻ£ŠĻ≠ha* 6.1.61‚Äď69). He combines high *advaita-siddhńĀnta* with the practice-oriented yogic idiom of the *Yoga-VńĀsiŠĻ£ŠĻ≠ha* (the integration is programmatic in [*Jńęvanmukti-Viveka* III‚ÄďIV](cite://vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/III-IV)); the *Pa√ĪcadaŇõńę* derives its enduring pedagogical role from this combination (Hiriyanna, *Outlines*, 1932, ch. on VivaraŠĻáa-Advaita).

engaged_works.title: Panchadashi
engaged_works.summary: Independent didactic *prakaraŠĻáa* in fifteen chapters, divided into three pentads (*pa√Īcakas*): *viveka-pa√Īcaka* (the *tattva-*, *mahńĀvńĀkya-*, *pa√ĪcakoŇõa-*, *dvaita-*, and *mahńĀvńĀkyńĀrtha-viveka* chapters of analysis); *dńępa-pa√Īcaka* (the *citra-*, *tŠĻõpti-*, *kŇęŠĻ≠astha-*, *dhyńĀna-*, and *nńĀŠĻ≠aka-dńępa* chapters of illumination); and *ńĀnanda-pa√Īcaka* (bliss). VidyńĀraŠĻáya works through the *avasthńĀ-traya* and *pa√Īca-koŇõa-viveka* of the UpaniŠĻ£ads, the analysis of the MahńĀvńĀkya, the integration of *avaccheda-* and *pratibimba-vńĀda* on the *jńęva*, the cosmological and psychological roles of *mńĀyńĀ* and avidyńĀ, the metaphysics of *jńęvanmukti*, and the experiential character of *brahmńĀnanda*. The verse is clear *anuŠĻ£ŠĻ≠ubh*; the illustrations are drawn from theatre, painting, and dream. The work has become the principal pedagogical *prakaraŠĻáa* of post-ŇöaŠĻÖkara Advaita and is read as a primer alongside ŇöaŠĻÖkara's *UpadeŇõa-SńĀhasrńę* in modern *VedńĀnta* curricula. It is foundational for the Advaita VedńĀnta articulated to general audiences from the eighteenth century onward.

engaged_works.title: Jivanmukti-Viveka
engaged_works.summary: Independent *prakaraŠĻáa* in five chapters devoted to the systematic exposition of *jńęvanmukti*, liberation in this very life. VidyńĀraŠĻáya treats it as a precise technical state, not a metaphor. AvidyńĀ has been removed by *brahma-j√ĪńĀna*; *prńĀrabdha-karma* sustains the body until exhausted; *vńĀsanńĀ-kŠĻ£aya* and *manonńĀŇõa*, drawn programmatically from the *Yoga-VńĀsiŠĻ£ŠĻ≠ha*, are required for the cessation of residual mental activity. He distinguishes *vidvat-sannyńĀsa* (renunciation following the rise of knowledge) from *vividiŠĻ£ńĀ-sannyńĀsa* (renunciation as a means to its rise) and defends the legitimacy of *jńęvanmukti* against opponents who would defer liberation to *videhamukti*. Yoga, *Yoga-VńĀsiŠĻ£ŠĻ≠ha*, and PńĀta√Ījala technical vocabulary are integrated into the Advaita framework. *Jńęvanmukti* is treated as the proximate goal of spiritual practice. The work is the principal Advaita reference on the topic and the source for much of the modern Advaita understanding of the relation between knowledge, action, and continuing embodiment.
engaged_works.source_status_note: Sanskrit text not on disk in clean form; the Subrahmanya Sastri 1935/1978 Adyar Library edition is image-only on archive.org. Sanskrit-IAST verbatim citations are demoted to secondary-attestation via Andrew Fort, Jivanmukti in Transformation (SUNY 1998) or Robert A. Goodding, The Jivanmuktiviveka of Vidyaranya (Brill 2013).

engaged_works.title: Vivarana-Prameya-Samgraha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored compendium of PrakńĀŇõńĀtman's VivaraŠĻáa.
engaged_works.summary: Topical compendium (*saŠĻÉgraha*) of the principal *prameyas* of PrakńĀŇõńĀtman's *Pa√ĪcapńĀdikńĀ-VivaraŠĻáa*, organised as a systematic *prameya*-by-*prameya* presentation rather than a running commentary. VidyńĀraŠĻáya extracts and re-presents the VivaraŠĻáa positions on Brahman as locus of avidyńĀ, the *bhńĀva-rŇępńĀ-anirvacanńęya-avidyńĀ* doctrine, the *bimba-pratibimba* analysis of the *jńęva*, the rejection of *j√ĪńĀna-karma-samuccaya*, and the function of the MahńĀvńĀkya. The work matters as the canonical hand-list of VivaraŠĻáa tenets in compact form. It makes PrakńĀŇõńĀtman's massive *VivaraŠĻáa* navigable for later students and is one of the standard references through which the VivaraŠĻáa positions enter post-fourteenth-century Advaita pedagogy and the *Advaita-Siddhi*-era polemics.

engaged_works.title: Sarva-Darshana-Samgraha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed to MńĀdhava (= VidyńĀraŠĻáya); modern scholarship accepts the ascription though some debate the exact compositional role.
engaged_works.summary: Doxographical *saŠĻÉgraha* presenting sixteen *darŇõanas* in ascending order of claimed approximation to truth, beginning with the CńĀrvńĀka and culminating in ŇöaŠĻÖkara's Advaita. Each chapter expounds a school in its own technical idiom: Bauddha, Jaina, RńĀmńĀnujńęya, PŇęrŠĻáapraj√Īa (Madhva), NakulńęŇõa-PńĀŇõupata, Ňöaiva, Pratyabhij√ĪńĀ, RaseŇõvara, AulŇękya (VaiŇõeŠĻ£ika), AkŠĻ£apńĀda (NyńĀya), Jaimini, PńĀŠĻáinńęya, SńĀŠĻÉkhya, PńĀta√Ījala, and finally ŇöńĀŠĻÖkara. The work is universally cited as a principal Sanskrit source for opposed-school positions. Institutionally it is the single most influential pre-modern Indian doxography. Methodologically it is a paradigm of *pŇęrvapakŠĻ£a*-fidelity: schools are presented in their own voice with their canonical citations before being relativised to the Advaita climax. Some modern scholars debate the exact compositional role of MńĀdhava as against his collaborators; the work is firmly within the VidyńĀraŠĻáya‚ÄďSńĀyaŠĻáa Vijayanagara scholarly project.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
cidńĀnandamayabrahmapratibimbasamanvitńĀ |
tamo-rajaŠł•-sattvaguŠĻáńĀ prakŠĻõtir dvividhńĀ ca sńĀ || 15 ||
sattvaŇõuddhyaviŇõuddhibhyńĀŠĻÉ mńĀyńĀvidye ca te mate |
mńĀyńĀbimbo vaŇõńękŠĻõtya tńĀŠĻÉ syńĀt sarvaj√Īa ńęŇõvaraŠł• || 16 ||

pada_analysis.gloss: endowed with the reflection of Brahman consisting of consciousness and bliss
having the guŠĻáas tamas, rajas, and sattva
through the purity and impurity of sattva

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: tamaŇõ ca rajaŇõ ca sattvaŠĻÉ ca guŠĻáńĀ yasmin sńĀ

verb_modality.note: Predicative optative used here in a definitional sense.

key_passages.english_close: PrakŠĻõti is endowed with the reflection of Brahman consisting of consciousness and bliss, and is possessed of the guŠĻáas tamas, rajas, and sattva. And that prakŠĻõti is twofold. Through the purity and the impurity of sattva, those two [parts of prakŠĻõti] are regarded as mńĀyńĀ and avidyńĀ [respectively]. The reflection in mńĀyńĀ, having brought that [mńĀyńĀ] under its control, becomes the omniscient ń™Ňõvara.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: primordial materiality or undifferentiated causal matrix
the pure-sattva causal power associated with ń™Ňõvara
ignorance, here the impure-sattva form of causal unknowing
the Lord, omniscient Brahman as causally related to the world

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is a position-establishing locus, not a detachable slogan: it gives VidyńĀraŠĻáya's compact VivaraŠĻáa mapping of twofold prakŠĻõti into mńĀyńĀ and avidyńĀ and explicitly links lordship to the mńĀyńĀ-reflection. Because the Sanskrit corpus on disk is scan-only, I prioritized passages whose wording could be securely cross-checked against the local English translation; this one is still foundational rather than opportunistic.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
yathńĀ mu√ĪjńĀd iveŠĻ£ńękńĀm ńĀtmńĀ yuktyńĀ samuddhŠĻõtaŠł• |
ŇõarńęratritayńĀd evaŠĻÉ paraŠĻÉ brahmaiva jńĀyate || 42 ||

pada_analysis.morphology: particle + acc sg fem (sandhi form: iva + iŠĻ£ńękńĀm)

pada_analysis.gloss: comes to be, is recognized as

verb_modality.note: Used in the sense of recognition or realization, not literal production.

key_passages.english_close: Just as the inner fibre is drawn out from mu√Īja grass. So too, when the self is extracted by reasoning from the triad of bodies, it [is recognized to] become nothing but the supreme Brahman.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the reed or grass from which the inner pith is carefully drawn out
the three bodies: gross, subtle, and causal

key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse is the payoff of the chapter's discriminative method: it states, in VidyńĀraŠĻáya's own pedagogical idiom, how the self is analytically separated from the layered body-complex and recognized as Brahman. It fairly represents his teaching because the Pa√ĪcadaŇõńę is meant to train exactly this style of viveka rather than merely assert identity abstractly.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tńĀbhyńĀŠĻÉ nirvicikitsńĀrthe cetasaŠł• sthńĀpitasya yat |
ekatńĀnattvam etad dhi nididhyńĀsanam ucyate || 54 ||
dhyńĀtŠĻõdhyńĀne parityajya kramńĀd dhyeyaikagocaram |
nivńĀtadńępavac cittaŠĻÉ samńĀdhir abhidhńęyate || 55 ||

pada_analysis.gloss: in the matter free of doubt
having the object of meditation alone as its range
like a lamp in a windless place

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: dhyeyaŠĻÉ eva eko gocaro yasya tat

key_passages.english_close: When, by those two [ŇõravaŠĻáa and manana], the mind has been established in the matter freed from doubt. The one-pointed continuity that ensues ‚ÄĒ this indeed is called nididhyńĀsana. Having left aside meditator and meditation, [and] step by step having only the object of meditation as its range. The mind, then, like a lamp in a windless place, is designated samńĀdhi.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: deep contemplative assimilation of the teaching already heard and reasoned through

key_passages.why_this_passage: VidyńĀraŠĻáya is not content with a bare claim that knowledge liberates; he specifies the internal sequence by which ŇõravaŠĻáa and manana mature into nididhyńĀsana and samńĀdhi. That makes this a representative passage for his synthesis of Advaita pedagogy with contemplative discipline, rather than an isolated yogic flourish.
